utterances: Mm-hmm.
What about trying to de-acidify or neutralize some of that acid in local water situations.
Is it possible?
It is possible in a very limited extent.
It's possible to - fish tank owners now, who keep coral reefs in their saltwater fish tanks dissolve calcium carbonate using carbon dioxide and put that resulting solution in their fish tank.
And we could do the same thing in a very limited bay or region of the ocean.
But the amount of effort it would take to do it on a large scale is incredibly huge, and it would be just simpler to focus on our energy system at that point.
So, going the way we are going now - and even if we met Kyoto standards and could not get the CO2 levels down, do you see - envision coral reefs existing in museums, aquaria, things like that?
Yeah.

I think that, you know, there might be a few marine reserves where we might be able to let them continue existing, sort of a Disney world of what - you know, so children can see what the oceans used to be like before we ignored all the warnings of the scientists.
But I'm hoping that people will wake up and respond to this issue with the kind of vigor that we need.
And you're optimistic about this?
I - let's just say that I'm hopeful, but maybe not completely optimistic.
Well, you know, I guess if we viewed them as treasures, like you say, and notice them, you know, melting like the ice caps - we don't see them very much below the water there, you know?
It's easy to see satellite pictures of melting ice caps but not of dying coral reef.
Yeah.
One problem is that humans respond very well to things that are in our sensory field.
We respond emotionally to things that we see with our eyes, and things like the Great Barrier Reef dying or these other Caribbean reefs dying, it's really an abstraction.

And we were able to respond to the - when the twin, you know, the World Trade Centers were bombed or the airplanes went into it because we have this visceral emotional reaction and we're willing to put it lots of resources to respond to those issues.
And we really need to somehow get ourselves to the point where we're responding to the carbon dioxide problem with the same vigor.
And, you know, how we move people there, I don't know.

But certainly, studies like ours that suggests that ecosystems that have existed on this planet without any problem for many, many tens of millions of years, because of what we do in the few tens of years, disappear.
The last time coral reef from the geologic record was when the dinosaurs became extinct.
And it took - the oceans, at that time, were acidified probably for a few tens of thousands of years.
But it took about 2 million years for the corals to come back and repopulate the coast of the planet.
And so, what we're doing over the next decades will affect life in the ocean for millions of years.
I mean, the question of, you know, it just seems to me incredibly unwise for us to be disturbing extremely complicated system work profoundly.
